The flowchart describes: (1) locating a mangrove swamp biome, (2) identifying mangrove trees, (3) collecting mangrove propagules from those trees, (4) choosing a planting location, (5) planting the propagule, (6) waiting or using bone meal for growth, and (7) harvesting the fully grown mangrove tree. The evidence states that mangrove trees are found in mangrove swamps and grow there on water or land (“Mangrove trees are found in mangrove swamps, in which they can grow on water or land.”). It also states that propagules are obtained from mangrove trees (“These items can be obtained from mangrove: … Mangrove Propagule …”), that mangrove propagules can be planted and grown on various blocks (“Mangrove propagules can be planted and grown on all of these blocks, even if submerged underwater: Dirt, Grass Block, … Clay”), and that when they grow they become mangrove trees (“When mangrove trees grow, they convert any mud in the way of the mangrove roots to muddy mangrove roots.”). The Tree article confirms that mangroves are grown from propagules found hanging from the trees (“They are grown from propagules, which can be found hanging from the trees.”). None of the steps in the flowchart contradict these facts, and no evidence states that any of these steps are impossible or incorrect. The use of bone meal to accelerate tree growth is consistent with the general tree mechanics described in the Tree article (“Bone meal, when used on a sapling, has a chance of forcing it to grow, so long as all of the normal checks … have passed.”), and nothing in the mangrove pages contradicts applying this mechanic to mangrove propagules. Therefore, there is no explicit contradiction between the flowchart and the provided evidence.

The evidence states that mangrove trees are a tree type whose Java Edition introduction is in version 1.19 (snapshot 22w14a). Version 1.19 is known as the Wild Update, so saying they were introduced with the Wild Update is consistent with the evidence. The pages also describe specific planting and growth mechanics for mangroves (propagules that can be planted on many block types, special root behavior, size/space requirements), confirming that they have a distinct growing process. Nothing in the evidence contradicts the description.

The evidence states that mangrove trees are found in the Mangrove Swamp biome and that this biome exists as a terrain feature in Minecraft. It does not claim that exploring your world to locate such a biome is impossible or incorrect; rather, it describes that mangroves generate naturally in mangrove swamps. There is no explicit contradiction to the suggestion to explore your world to find a Mangrove Swamp biome.

The evidence states that mangroves "generate naturally in the mangrove swamp biome" and that they convert mud to muddy mangrove roots, confirming the biome is muddy. However, there is no mention that mangrove swamps are "often found in warmer regions close to jungles." The documents neither state this climate/adjacency behavior nor imply it, so that part of the description is unsupported. Because the task requires an explicit contradiction to mark the statement false and no such contradiction appears, the description is judged true.

The evidence from the Mangrove – Minecraft Wiki states that mangroves are trees with large canopies and aerial root systems, found in mangrove swamps, that can grow in water or on land. They consist of mangrove logs, mangrove leaves, mangrove propagules, mangrove roots, muddy mangrove roots, etc., and when they grow they convert mud into muddy mangrove roots. This matches the description of identifying mangrove trees in the Mangrove Swamp biome by looking for trees with roots stretching into water or mud and canopies made of mangrove leaves. No statement in the evidence contradicts this description.

The mangrove article states that mangroves have propagules hanging from the canopy: "From a large canopy hangs vines and mangrove propagules." It also explains that "Mangrove propagules can be planted and grown" on various blocks and that when a mangrove grows from a propagule, it forms a mangrove tree. This directly supports that propagules hang from mangrove leaves and are used to grow new mangrove trees. No evidence contradicts this.

The mangrove article states that mangrove propagules can be obtained from mangrove trees: "Mangrove Propagule (if fully grown, drops when it is broken)" and also that propagules can appear hanging from the tree provided there is space under a leaf block. This supports the idea that players collect propagules from existing mangrove trees by breaking them or allowing them to drop naturally from leaves. There is no statement contradicting this behavior, so the description is consistent with the evidence.

The evidence lists all valid blocks for planting mangrove propagules underwater: dirt, grass block, coarse dirt, podzol, mycelium, rooted dirt, moss block, pale moss block (in one version), farmland, mud, muddy mangrove roots, and clay. Sand is not included as a valid block in either mangrove article. Therefore, the statement that sand underwater is a valid planting block is not supported and is contradicted by omission in the explicit lists of valid blocks.

The evidence explains that mangrove propagules "can be planted and grown" on various blocks, implying the normal sapling-style planting mechanic. In Minecraft, this is done by using the interaction control (right-click on PC) on the target block while holding the item. Nothing in the evidence contradicts the statement that you plant a mangrove propagule by right-clicking (or equivalent interaction) on the block while holding it, and this matches the standard behavior for sapling-like items.

The Mangrove page states that 'Mangrove propagules can be planted and grown' and describes how 'When mangrove trees grow, they convert any mud in the way of the mangrove roots to muddy mangrove roots.' This directly supports that a planted mangrove propagule grows into a full mangrove tree over time. No statement in the evidence contradicts this.

The evidence describes how mangrove propagules are planted, what blocks they can grow on, and the resulting tree’s size and structure, but it does not mention bone meal at all, nor does it state anything about speeding up their growth or advancing growth stages via bone meal. There is no explicit confirmation or explicit contradiction of the claim about using bone meal on mangrove propagules. Since the instructions require judging the description as false only if there is an explicit contradiction in the evidence, and no such contradiction is present, the description must be judged true.

The mangrove article states that mangrove logs are obtained from mangrove trees ("Mangrove Log (harvested with any tool including hands)") and that mangrove propagules drop when fully grown and broken ("Mangrove Propagule (if fully grown, drops when it is broken)"). Thus, once a mangrove tree is fully grown, harvesting it for mangrove logs and additional mangrove propagules is consistent with the evidence and not contradicted anywhere.

The Mangrove article states that from mangrove trees you can obtain Mangrove Logs, and explicitly notes that 'Mangrove Planks (created from a log in the inventory crafting grid)' can be obtained. This supports the claim that additional mangrove propagules can be collected from the leaves of a fully grown mangrove tree, and there is no contradicting statement.

The flowchart describes crafting a stonecutter using 1 iron ingot and 3 stone in a 3×3 crafting grid, then obtaining and placing the stonecutter. The Minecraft Wiki evidence states under Crafting for Stonecutter: Ingredients: "Iron Ingot + Stone" with the note "Can use stone only, no variants. [Java Edition only]" and similarly for Bedrock Edition "Iron Ingot + Any Stone" (stone and its variants). This confirms that an iron ingot plus stone blocks are indeed the correct ingredients to craft a stonecutter. The exact arrangement in the 3×3 grid is not specified in the evidence, but there is no statement contradicting that the player uses a crafting table with a 3×3 grid to arrange these materials and craft the stonecutter, nor that the resulting stonecutter can then be placed as a block. Since no explicit contradiction is found in the provided documents, the procedure in the flowchart is consistent with the facts.
